design_goal: Give security admins a structured, five-section Red Team workspace — with a composable attack builder, conversation review surface, and approval-gated campaign flow — that makes active social engineering simulation as accessible as passive phishing simulation.
constraints: Node-based editor scoped to linear sequences only in v1 — no branching attack paths
Complicity marking requires a confirmation step — not single-click due to HR implications
Request-start model requires a configurable Red Team Approver RBAC role
AEP library must be Dune-seeded from day one — empty library hard-blocks campaign creation
Channel availability is tenant-scoped — unconfigured channels shown as locked, not hidden
Existing Simulations → Smishing nav path must not be disrupted
selected_strategy: Option A — Dedicated top-level Red Team nav section with five sub-sections
selected_strategy_rationale: Red team (active adversarial) and passive simulation are distinct operational contexts requiring different admin mindsets. Top-level nav makes this distinction clear, avoids polluting the Simulations section with red team complexity, and accommodates five sub-sections cleanly under a tab bar without exceeding DS-recommended nav depth.

alternative_strategies.name: Option B — Red Team as sub-section under Simulations
alternative_strategies.summary: Add Red Team as a sub-nav item under Simulations alongside Email Phishing and Smishing
alternative_strategies.rejected_because: Five nested sub-sections under Simulations → Red Team creates three nav levels, exceeding DS-recommended depth. Conflates active and passive simulation operational contexts.

alternative_strategies.name: Option C — Red Team as a campaign type in existing Simulations wizard
alternative_strategies.summary: Add a Red Team campaign type at wizard step 1; VEP and attack selection become wizard steps
alternative_strategies.rejected_because: Does not accommodate AEP Library and Attack Library as standalone management sections. Undersells the product and creates an unmaintainable wizard with excessive conditional paths.

tradeoffs: Admins cannot see all simulation types (email + smishing + red team) in a single campaign list without a future cross-type reporting view
Top-level nav addition requires product/design alignment on global nav IA
Red team campaigns produce conversation threads, not click rates — cross-type comparisons must be built as explicit future features
assumptions: VEPs are linear sequences in v1 (not branching decision trees)
Complicity marking is manually performed by admin with optional system-suggested flags
Voice channel in v1 is human-executed — platform provides operator script guide, does not initiate/record calls
A configurable Red Team Approver RBAC role exists or will be created
Dune ships a library of pre-built VEPs covering common social engineering scenarios at launch
Channel availability (WhatsApp, Viber) is tenant-scoped and gated in the attack editor
open_issues: VEP branching scope — linear vs. branching for v1 must be confirmed before VEP creation form is designed
Approver RBAC model — new permission level must be confirmed with Eng before campaign wizard Step 6 is finalized
Voice channel automation scope — human-logged vs. automated for v1 determines node configuration design
Complicity auto-suggestion — NLP/keyword detection scope must be confirmed before thread detail is designed
Dashboard data contracts — time window, metrics, and refresh cadence must be confirmed before handoff
Channel provider provisioning flow — how admins enable WhatsApp/Viber is a Settings-level dependency
risks: VEP cold-start — if Dune does not seed VEPs at launch, campaign creation is immediately blocked
Node editor scope creep — branching paths require stateful orchestration engine not scoped for v1
Complicity marking inconsistency — judgment-based outcome requires inline guidance to improve inter-admin consistency
Approval dead-end — if no approver is configured, Request Start CTA becomes a dead end
Channel availability gaps — admins can build attacks using channels their tenant cannot send through
new_patterns_required: Conversation script editor (sequential attacker/response node pairs) — no DS equivalent; requires DS review
Node-based canvas editor (linear attack sequence builder with channel nodes) — no DS equivalent; requires DS review
VEP preview drawer (shows conversation script as a simulated exchange thread) — extension of existing drawer pattern; DS review recommended
next_actions: Resolve VEP branching scope with PM before designing VEP creation form
Confirm approver RBAC model with Eng before designing campaign wizard Step 6
Design AEP Library section first — fewest unresolved dependencies, establishes pattern for Attack Library
Design conversation thread detail and complicity marking — highest-risk novel UI pattern
Scope node-based editor linearity constraint with Eng before opening Figma on Attack Library
Review Stillsuit DS v2 token set for complicity outcome state — new semantic tokens may be needed
